title: Chirality Transfer to the Centrosymmetric Magnetic Sublattice in the Hybrid Perovskite (R)-/(S)-3-Fluoropyrrolidinium Copper(II) Chloride
authors: Zheng Zhang, Mingyu Xu, Jose L. Gonzalez Jimenez, Stephen Zhang, Weiwei Xie, Xianghan Xu, Daniel B. Straus
abstract: Incorporating chiral organic cations into organic-inorganic hybrid materials has been shown to enable the inorganic sublattice to display chiroptical properties. We report a new two-dimensional magnetic (S=1/2) chiral metal halide material, (R)- and (S)-$(C_4H_9FN)_2CuCl_4$ (where $(C_4H_9FN)^+$ is 3-fluoropyrrolidinium), which consists of Cu-Cl inorganic layers separated by $(C_4H_9FN)^+$ organic cations. The presence of the chiral $(C_4H_9FN)^+$ organic cation induces formation of chiral magnetic order, even though the inorganic sublattice itself is structurally centrosymmetric. We also report the racemic variant, containing an equal amount of (R)- and (S)- cations, which shows no evidence of chiral magnetic order. When the magnetic susceptibility is measured perpendicular to inorganic Cu-Cl layer propagation direction, an antiferromagnetic phase transition at Néel temperature $T_N = 2.23~K$ is observed in both the chiral and racemic materials, and the existence of the magnetic phase transition is supported by specific heat capacity measurements. Field-induced magnetic chirality is observed through the existence of a second-order magnetoelectric effect in the chiral variant, while no magnetoelectric signal is observed for the racemic material, indicating the absence of magnetic chirality. Our findings demonstrate that materials exhibiting chiral magnetic order can be created through the incorporation of a chiral cation into an organic-inorganic hybrid magnetic material, potentially allowing for the design of tailored materials that combine chiral magnetism with other desirable optical and electronic properties that come from structural chirality.
summary: This paper demonstrates that chirality can be transferred from organic molecules to an inorganic magnetic lattice in a hybrid perovskite. While the copper-chloride sublattice is structurally centrosymmetric, the presence of chiral cations induces chiral magnetic order. This is experimentally verified through the observation of a second-order magnetoelectric effect in the enantiopure crystals, which is absent in the racemic mixture. This finding provides a method for designing new materials with tailored chiral magnetic and optical properties.
main_problem: Investigating the mechanism of chirality transfer from chiral organic cations to a structurally centrosymmetric (achiral) inorganic magnetic sublattice in a hybrid perovskite.
main_result: The chiral organic cations induce chiral magnetic order in the achiral Cu-Cl sublattice, evidenced by a second-order magnetoelectric effect present only in the enantiopure forms.
method: Single-crystal X-ray diffraction, Circular Dichroism (CD) spectroscopy, magnetic susceptibility, specific heat capacity, and magnetoelectric current measurements.
model_or_system: A 2D magnetic S=1/2 metal halide perovskite, (R)- and (S)-3-fluoropyrrolidinium copper(II) chloride, consisting of Cu-Cl inorganic layers separated by chiral organic cations.
key_observables: Néel temperature (TN), magnetoelectric signal, circular dichroism (CD) spectra, magnetic susceptibility (DC and AC), and specific heat capacity.
important_parameters: Néel temperature TN = 2.23 K, spin S=1/2, space groups P212121 (chiral) and P21/c (racemic).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the observed magnetoelectric effect is a direct consequence of the induced toroidal moment from structural chirality.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the crystal structures of the (R), (S), and racemic variants; Figure 6 displays AC susceptibility components; Figure 7 shows the magnetoelectric signal in chiral vs. racemic samples.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the chirality transfer problem, describes the synthesis and structural characterization of the hybrid perovskite, presents magnetic and optical measurements to demonstrate chirality transfer, and concludes with the observation of the magnetoelectric effect.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Crystal structures of (a) and (b) (R)- and (S)-(C4H9FN)2CuCl4, and (c) and (d) racemic (C4H9FN)2CuCl4. Hydrogen atoms are omitted for clarity.
Figure 2. (a) UV-Vis absorption and (b) CD spectra of (R)-, (S)-(C4H9FN)2CuCl4 and racemic (C4H9FN)2CuCl4 thin films.

Figure 3. Plot of the inverse susceptibility (𝜒−𝜒0)−1 versus temperature for powder samples of (R)-, (S)-, and racemic (C4H9FN)2CuCl4. Curie-Weiss fits are shown as solid lines with extrapolations of the fits as dashed lines.
Figure 4. Plots of the magnetic susceptibility and magnetic specific heat capacity data for (a), (c), (e) chiral (C4H9FN)2CuCl4 and (b), (d), (f) racemic (C4H9FN)2CuCl4 single crystals.
Figure 5. Plots of the magnetic moment vs. magnetic field H (at T=1.8 K) for (a) chiral and (b) racemic (C4H9FN)2CuCl4 single crystals. The blue circle in panel a is likely a spin-flop transition.

Figure 6. In-phase (χ’) component of AC susceptibility for (a) (R)-(C4H9FN)2CuCl4 and (b) racemic (C4H9FN)2CuCl4. Out-of-phase (χ’’) component of AC susceptibility component for (c) (R)-(C4H9FN)2CuCl4 and (d) racemic (C4H9FN)2CuCl4.

Figure 7. Magnetoelectric data collected at 2 K on (a) chiral and (b) racemic (C4H9FN)2CuCl4 crystals. The magnetic field is applied along the in-plane (110) direction and the out-of-plane magnetoelectric current is collected. 3. Conclusion
